topic: Areas of Inquiry: Music Technology in the Electronic and Digital Age
subject: music

items.correct: A cadence ending on chord V, creating a sense of incompleteness.
items.misconception: A cadence that sounds wrong or is performed badly.
items.random_wrong: A cadence using only major chords.
items.why: Students think 'imperfect' means flawed. It's a technical term for an unfinished-sounding cadence - any chord moving to V leaves the music suspended, awaiting resolution.

items.q: What does the time signature 6/8 indicate?
items.correct: Two beats per bar, each beat divided into three (compound duple).
items.misconception: Six beats per bar, all equal in stress.
items.why: Students count 6/8 as six equal beats. In practice it's felt as TWO dotted-crotchet beats subdivided into three quavers each - compound duple, not simple sextuple.

items.correct: Two or more independent melodic lines sounding together.
items.misconception: A single melody with chord accompaniment.
items.why: Students confuse polyphony with thick texture or homophony. Polyphony specifically means INDEPENDENT MELODIES - like a fugue. Melody + chords is HOMOPHONY, not polyphony.
Correct answer: Leon Theremin. “Robert Moog” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Whose 1956 Gesang der Junglinge mixed boy's voice with electronics?
items.why: It's Stockhausen. “Berio” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year was Ableton Live first released?
items.why: The real figure is 2001 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
